text: Key Points of Arrays in Java An array is a fixed-size collection of elements of the same data type. Array access is fast because elements are accessed using index values. Array elements are stored in contiguous memory locations. Array size is decided at creation time and cannot be changed later. Arrays use zero-based indexing (first element at index `0`). Arrays in Java are objects, not primitive variables. The `length` property gives the size of the array (not a method). Arrays are created using the `new` keyword. Java automatically initializes array elements with default values. Primitive arrays store values directly, whereas object arrays store references to objects.

SET 1: PRIMITIVE ARRAY (int, double, char, etc.) Declare Array <type> < arrayName >[]; e.g. int x[]; Allocate Memory to array < arrayName > = new <type>[<size>]; e.g. x = new int[100]; Define and allocate memory together <type> < arrayName >[] = new <type>[<size>]; e.g. int x[] = new int[100]; Initialize Array: Option 1 < arrayName >[<subscript>] = <value>; e.g. x[5] = 100; Using loop: For (int i = 0; i < 100; i ++) x[ i ] = <value>; Initialize Array: Option 2 <type> < arrayName >[] = { <list of values> }; e.g. int x[] = {12, 13, 14}; Size of array int i = x.length ; SET 2: OBJECT ARRAY (class type) Declare Array < className > < arrayName >[]; e.g. Book books[]; Allocate Memory to array (references only) < arrayName > = new < className >[<size>]; e.g. books = new Book[3]; Define and allocate memory together < className > < arrayName >[] = new < className >[<size>]; e.g. Book books[] = new Book[3]; Initialize Array: Option 1 (IMPORTANT object creation required) < arrayName >[<index>] = new < className >(); books[0] = new Book(); books[1] = new Book(); Assign values to object fields: books[0].title = "Java"; books[1].title = "OOP"; Initialize Array: Option 2 (array + objects together) < className > < arrayName >[] = { new < className >(), new < className >() }; e.g.Book books[] = { new Book(), new Book() }; Size of array int i = books.length ;

Write a Java program that: Declares an integer array Allocates memory for the array (size 5) Initializes the array elements with any integer values Prints all array elements using a loop Create a class named IntArrayDemo . Define the main method as the program entry point. Inside main, declare an integer array variable. Allocate memory to the array for 5 integer elements. Initialize the array elements with integer values using a loop. Use a loop to access each array element. Print each element to the console. Declare Int Array : int[] x ; Allocate memory: x = new int[5]; Initialize Array: for (int i =0; i < x.length;i ++) X[ i ]= i + 1; mportantYears = new int[] {800, 1066, 1492,};

class IntArrayDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { int[] x = new int[5]; for (int i = 0; i < x.length ; i ++) { x[ i ] = i + 1; } for (int i = 0; i < x.length ; i ++) { System.out.println (x[ i ]); } } }

Primitive Array Specific Points Primitive arrays store actual values (int, double, char, etc.). Only one `new` operation is required. Default values: `int` `0` `double` `0.0` ` boolean ` `false` e.g int[] a = new int[3]; Object Array Specific Points Object arrays store references to objects, not the objects themselves. Creating an object array does not create objects automatically. Each object must be created separately using `new`. Default value for object references is `null`. Accessing an object before creation causes ` NullPointerException `. Example: Book[] books = new Book[2]; books[0] = new Book();

class TestArray { String title ; public static void main(String args []) { TestArray [] myBooks = new TestArray [2]; int x = 0; myBooks [0].title = "b1"; myBooks [1].title = "b2"; while(x < 2) { System. out .println ( "book "+ myBooks [x].title ); x++ ; } } class Hobbits { String name ; public static void main(String args []) { Hobbits[] h = new Hobbits[2] ; int z = 0 ; while (z < 2) { z = z + 1 ; h[z] = new Hobbits() ; h[z].name ="H" + z ; System. out .println ("name "+ h[z].name ); } } }

Program Requirements Object Array Declares an object array Allocates memory for the array (size 5) Creates objects inside the array Prints all object values using a loop Low Level Design Object Array Create a class named Book Add one data member title Create a class named ObjectArrayDemo Define the main method Inside main, declare an object array Allocate memory for 5 objects Create objects using a loop Assign values to object fields Use a loop to print object values Class Creation class Book { String title; } Declare Object Array Book[] books; Allocate Memory books = new Book[5]; Initialize Array, Assign and Read Values for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { books[ i ] = new Book(); books[ i ].title = "Book " + ( i + 1); } for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { System.out.println (books[ i ].title); }

class Book { String title; } class ObjectArrayDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { Book[] books; books = new Book[5]; for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { books[ i ] = new Book(); books[ i ].title = "Book " + ( i + 1); } for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { System.out.println (books[ i ].title); } } }
Abstract class Bike{ Abstract void run(); } Class Honda extends Bike{ void run() { System.out.println ( run safely ); } Public static void main() { Bike obj = new Honda() ; obj.run () ; }

Integers Range Overflow & Underflow int myMinIntValue = Integer.MIN_VALUE ; int myMaxIntValue = Integer.MAX_VALUE ; Will this compile Int myMinIntValue = ( Integer.MAX_VALUE + 1); Int myMinIntValue = (2147486347+ 1); Int myMinIntValue =2_147_483_647 ;

DATA TYPE , Width ( in bits), Min Value, Max Value Byte , Short, Int , Long System.out.println ("Integer value range (" + Integer.MIN_VALUE + " to " + Integer.MAX_VALUE + ")") ; System.out.println ("Byte value range (" + Byte.MIN_VALUE + " to " + Byte.MAX_VALUE + ")") ; System.out.println ("Short value range (" + Short.MIN_VALUE + " to " + Short.MAX_VALUE + ")") ; System.out.println ("Long value range (" + Long.MIN_VALUE + " to " + Long.MAX_VALUE + ")") ; System.out.println ("Integer size (" + Integer.SIZE ) ; System.out.println ("Byte size (" + Byte.SIZE ) ; System.out.println ("Short size (" + Short.SIZE ) ; System.out.println ("Long size (" + Long.SIZE ) ;
byte myNewByteValue = ( myMinByteValue / 2 ); byte myNewByteValue = ( -128 / 2) ; byte myNewByteValue = (byte) ( myMinByteValue / 2 );

Recap: In Java, int is the default data type used in arithmetic operations, even when working with byte, short, or long. To explicitly treat a value as long, we must append L to the literal (e.g., 100L). Java automatically applies overflow and underflow when a value exceeds the minimum or maximum range of a data type. In expressions, smaller data types (byte, short) are promoted to int, which can cause compilation errors during assignment. For example, short s = s / 2; results in an error because the expression evaluates to int. To resolve this, we must use explicit type casting (e.g., short s = (short)(s / 2);).

Floating-Point Types: float & double In Java, double is the default floating-point data type . Any decimal value like 5.5 is treated as a double by default. To store a value in a float, we must explicitly add F or f float f = 5.5F; In arithmetic expressions, float values are promoted to double , just like byte and short are promoted to int. Because of this, assignments such as: float f = 5.0 / 2; cause a compile-time error , since the result is a double. To fix this, we must either: use F literals, or apply explicit type casting : float f = (float)(5.0 / 2); Floating-point numbers do not store exact decimal values . This can lead to rounding errors : System.out.println (0.1 + 0.2); // 0.30000000000000004 Because of higher precision, double is preferred in most real-world calculations.

float a = 5.5; - Fix this float f = 5 / 2; -- Why is result not 2.5. Fix it float f = 1F / 3F; double d = 1.0 / 3.0; System.out.println (f); System.out.println (d); Which is safer for scientific calculations Why does Java promote Double and int ? Int result = 5 ; Result = result 5.5 ; Result = (int) (result 5.5) ;

`char` in Java is 2 bytes (16 bits) Java uses Unicode (UTF-16), not ASCII A `char` stores a single character, enclosed in single quotes char c = 'A'; Internally, a `char` is stored as a numeric Unicode value Because it is numeric, `char` participates in arithmetic Java promotes `char` to `int` in expressions char c = 'A'; System.out.println (c); // A System.out.println ((int)c); // 65

` boolean ` represents logical truth It can have only two values: true or false ``` Size is JVM-dependent (not defined in bytes) ` boolean ` cannot be cast to or from numbers Used mainly in: Conditions Loops Logical expressions boolean isJavaFun = true; boolean result = (5 > 3); System.out.println (result); if (true) { System.out.println ("This always runs"); } ``` > ` boolean ` is not data it is a decision

char c = 'A'; System.out.println (c); System.out.println ((int)c); char heart = '\u2764'; System.out.println (heart); Compile or no : char c = 65; char c = 'A' + 1; char c = (char)('A' + 1); boolean a = true; boolean b = false; System.out.println (a && b); System.out.println (a || b); System.out.println (!a); int age = 18; boolean canVote = age >= 18; if ( canVote ) { System.out.println ("Eligible to vote"); }

`String` is a class, not a primitive data type. But Java treats it specially (syntax + operators) String is Immutable String s = "Java"; - created in string constant pool s = s + " OOP"; new String()` Creates a New Object String a = "Java"; String b = new String("Java"); String a = "Test"; String b = "Test"; String c = new String("Test"); System.out.println (a == b); System.out.println (a == c); System.out.println ( a.equals (c));

`+` Operator Works with String & Numbers If any operand is a String, `+` means concatenation Evaluation is left to right System.out.println (10 + 20 + "Java"); // 30Java System.out.println ("Java" + 10 + 20); // Java1020 ``` Once a String appears, everything becomes a String Try + with char String.out.println ( A + B) String.out.println ( + A + B)
Polymorphism refers to a programming language's ability to process objects depending on their class.
Operators

Comments: Single-Line Comment // This is a single-line comment Multi-Line Comment /* This is a multi-line comment */ Documentation Comment (Intro Only) /** * This is a documentation comment */ (Used later with JavaDocs )

Java Data Types & Type Promotion Key Takeaways `int` is the default data type for integer arithmetic in Java `byte`, `short`, and `char` are automatically promoted to `int` in expressions Overflow and underflow are allowed silently in Java (no runtime error) Explicit type casting is required when assigning promoted results back to smaller types Floating-Point Types: `float` vs `double` `double` is the default floating-point type in Java Decimal literals like `5.5` are treated as `double` by default `float` values must use `F` or `f` suffix Floating-point arithmetic can produce rounding errors `double` is preferred for precision-critical and scientific calculations Type Promotion Rules (Why Errors Occur) Integer division (`5 / 2`) results in an integer, not a decimal Mixed-type expressions are promoted to the wider type Assignments may fail at compile time due to implicit widening + strict assignment rules Casting is required to make conversions explicit and safe

`char` in Java `char` is 2 bytes (16-bit) and uses Unicode (UTF-16) Can participate in arithmetic operations Automatically promoted to `int` in expressions Supports Unicode characters using escape sequences (`\ uXXXX `) ` boolean ` in Java Represents logical decisions, not numeric data Only two values: `true` and `false` Cannot be cast to or from numeric types Commonly used in Conditions Loops Logical expressions `String` in Java `String` is a class, not a primitive Strings are immutable String literals are stored in the String Constant Pool `new String()` always creates a new object `==` compares references, `.equals()` compares content

`+` Operator Behavior : Works with both numbers and strings If any operand is a String, `+` performs concatenation Expression evaluation happens left to right Once a String appears, the rest of the expression becomes a String Polymorphism (Intro Level) Polymorphism allows different behavior for the same method call Behavior depends on the object type, not reference type Operators Arithmetic Relational Logical Assignment operators Comment types: `//` Single-line /* */Multi-line `/ ** */` Documentation (used by JavaDocs )

public class FirstClass { public static void main(String[] args ) { System. out .println ("Hello"); } } JVM looks for an exact method signature. public JVM must access it static JVM doesn t create an object void nothing returned to JVM ; System.exit (0); // 0 = normal termination String[] args command-line input Class can have: Fields Methods Constructor Blocks Nested Classes

Class Circle { double x,y ; //center coordinates Double r ; // radius Double circumference() { return 2*3.14*r } Double area() { return (22/7)r*r ; } Class CircleDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { Circle c = new Circle() ; c.X = 0.0; c.Y = 0.0; c.r =5.0 ; System.out.println ( circ + c.circumrference ()); System.out.println ( area + c.area ()); }

Constructor Class Circle { double x,y ; //center coordinates Double r ; // radius Double circumference() { return 2*3.14*r } Double area() { return (22/7)r*r ; } Circle (double a, double b, double c) { x = a ; y = b ; r = c ; } Constructor can have args Return type is null Method name should be same as class

Constructor Overload Overloading is achieved by: Changing the number of parameters , or Changing the type of parameters class Student { String name; int age; // Constructor 1: No arguments Student() { name = "Unknown"; age = 0; } // Constructor 2: One argument Student(String n) { name = n; age = 0; } // Constructor 3: Two arguments Student(String n, int a) { name = n; age = a; } void display() { System.out.println (name + " " + age); } public static void main(String[] args ) { Student s1 = new Student(); Student s2 = new Student("Rahul"); Student s3 = new Student("Rahul", 20); s1.display(); s2.display(); s3.display(); } }

Design and develop a Java program to model a Bank Account using constructor overloading . The program should allow creation of bank account objects with different initial information depending on what data is available at the time of account creation. The program should support the following scenarios: Create a bank account without providing any details , where default values are assigned. Create a bank account by providing account holder name only . Create a bank account by providing account holder name and initial balance . Each scenario must use a different constructor of the same class.

Create a class named BankAccount Declare instance variables: Account holder name Account balance Constructor Design Constructor 1 (No arguments) Assign default values to account holder name and balance Constructor 2 (One argument) Accept account holder name Initialize name with provided value Set balance to default value Constructor 3 (Two arguments) Accept account holder name and initial balance Initialize both values using parameters
This variable class BankAccount { String accountHolder ; double balance; BankAccount (String accountHolder , double balance) { this.accountHolder = accountHolder ; this.balance = balance; } }

class BankAccount { String name; double balance; // Constructor 1 BankAccount () { this("Not Provided", 0.0); // calls Constructor 3 } // Constructor 2 BankAccount (String name) { this(name, 0.0); // calls Constructor 3 } // Constructor 3 (centralized initialization) BankAccount (String name, double balance) { this.name = name; this.balance = balance; } void display() { System.out.println (name + " " + balance); } } This() can be used in constructor only Should be first line

if (condition) { // runs if condition is true } else { // runs if condition is false } Condition must be Boolean int age = 20; if (age >= 18) { System.out.println ("Eligible to vote"); } else { System.out.println ("Not eligible to vote"); } Concept : If Else Condition

public class HelloName { public static void main(String[] args ) { if ( args.length == 0) { System.out.println ("Error: Please provide your name"); } else { System.out.println ("Hello " + args [0]); } } } Check Args in Hello World

public class FinalClass { public static void main(String[] args ) { if ( args.length == 0) { System. out .println ("Error: Please provide your name"); } else if ( args.length == 1) { System. out .println ("Hello " + args [0]); } else if ( args.length == 2) { System. out .println ("Hello " + args [0] + " " + args [1]); } } } Multiple Args in Command Line
if (condition1) { // statements } else if (condition2) { // statements } else if (condition3) { // statements } else { // default statements }

BufferedReader Package: `java.io` Used with ` InputStreamReader ` Reads input line by line Faster than Scanner Requires manual type conversion 3. Command Line Arguments Uses `String[] args ` in `main` Input passed while running the program No runtime input 4. Console Class Package: `java.io` Reads input securely (e.g., passwords) Not supported in some IDEs

Scanner sc = new Scanner(System. in ); System. out .print ("Enter your name: "); String name = sc.nextLine (); Scanner is a built-in Java class used to read input from different sources such as keyboard input, files, and strings It belongs to the package java.util When used with System.in, it reads standard input (keyboard input)
Static & Instance / Member Variables

Getter ( getX ) and Setter ( setX ) methods are public methods used to read and update private instance variables. Naming convention: getVariableName () returns the value, and setVariableName (value) updates it (camelCase, starts with get / set). They support encapsulation by hiding direct access to class data. Setters allow validation and business rules before modifying values. Getters provide controlled, read-only access to internal state. They improve code maintainability and flexibility without changing external code. Widely used in real-world applications like banking, user profiles, and configuration systems. Form the backbone of JavaBeans, frameworks, and APIs (Spring, Hibernate, etc.).

class BankAccount { // STATIC VARIABLE (shared by all accounts) static String bankName = "State Bank of India"; // PRIVATE INSTANCE VARIABLES (unique per account) private int accountNumber ; private double balance; // SETTER METHODS (to initialize/update instance variables) public void setAccountNumber (int accNo ) { accountNumber = accNo ; } public void setBalance (double amount) { if (amount >= 0) { balance = amount; } else { System.out.println ("Invalid balance amount"); } } // GETTER METHODS (to read instance variables) public int getAccountNumber () { return accountNumber ; } public double getBalance () { return balance; } } public class BankDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { BankAccount acc1 = new BankAccount (); BankAccount acc2 = new BankAccount (); acc1.setAccountNumber(101); acc1.setBalance(5000); acc2.setAccountNumber(102); acc2.setBalance(10000); System.out.println ("Bank Name: " + BankAccount.bankName ); System.out.println ("Account: " + acc1.getAccountNumber() + " Balance: " + acc1.getBalance()); System.out.println ("Account: " + acc2.getAccountNumber() + " Balance: " + acc2.getBalance()); } }
Parse String to int does not work Java converts primitives using casting, strings using wrapper class parsing methods, and invalid parsing is handled using NumberFormatException .
For loop use with array

java for ( dataType variable : collectionOrArray ) { // statements } Explanation: dataType type of elements in the array or collection variable holds one element at a time collectionOrArray array or collection to iterate over Automatically moves to the next element Best used when only reading values, not modifying indices Example: java int[] nums = {1, 2, 3}; for (int n : nums ) { System.out.println (n); }

import java.lang.reflect.Array ; class SimpleStartup { private int[] locationCells ; private int numOfHits = 0 ; public void setLocationCells ( int[] locs) { locationCells = locs ; } public String checkYourself (int guess) { String resultI = "miss" ; for (int cell : locationCells ) { if (guess == cell) { resultI ="hit" ; numOfHits ++ ; break ; } //end if if ( numOfHits == locationCells.length ){ resultI = "kill" ; } //end if } // end for System. out .println ( resultI ) ; return ( resultI ) ; } //end method } //end class public class SimplaStartupTest { public static void main(String[] args ){ SimpleStartup dot = new SimpleStartup () ; int[] locations = {2,3,4} ; int userGuess ; dot.setLocationCells (locations); System. out .println ("Enter Guess"); try { userGuess = Integer. parseInt ( args [0]); } catch ( NumberFormatException e) { System. out .println ("enter valid int") ; return ; } catch ( ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) { System. out .println ("enter parameter") ; return ; } String result = dot.checkYourself ( userGuess ) ; String testResult = "failed" ; if ( result.equals ("hit")) { testResult = "passed" ; } System. out .println ( testResult ); } }
Access Control & Inheritance

class A { void msg () { System . out .println ( "hello" ); } } class Outer { private class Inner { void show () { System . out .println ( "Inside Inner" ); } } void accessInner () { Inner obj = new Inner(); obj .show (); } } class testAccess { public static void main ( String args []) { // A obj = new A(); // obj.msg(); Outer o = new Outer() ; o .accessInner (); } }

Inheritance is an OOP mechanism where one class (child / subclass) acquires properties and methods of another class (parent / superclass). It is implemented in Java using the extends keyword. It represents an is-a relationship (e.g., Car is a Vehicle). It promotes code reusability by avoiding duplication. The child class can use, extend, or override methods of the parent class. It improves maintainability and extensibility of code. Java supports single inheritance for classes to avoid ambiguity. Access to parent members depends on access modifiers (public, protected, etc.).

class Vehicle { void start() { System.out.println ("Vehicle is starting"); } } class Car extends Vehicle { void drive() { start(); // using parent method } } class Vehicle { void start() { System.out.println ("Vehicle is starting"); } } class Car extends Vehicle { void playMusic () { System.out.println ("Playing music"); } } class Vehicle { void start() { System.out.println ("Vehicle is starting"); } } class Car extends Vehicle { @Override void start() { System.out.println ("Car starts with key ignition"); } }

Part 1: Parent Class and Main Class 1. Create a class named ` BasicPhone `. Declare a private variable named `brand` of type `String`. Declare a public method named ` makeCall ()` that prints exactly: Making a call using basic phone network with brand + brand 2. Create a class named ` TestPhone ` containing the `main()` method. Create an object of ` BasicPhone `. Call the ` makeCall ()` method.

class BasicPhone { private String brand = "iPhone" ; protected void makeCall (){ System. out .println ("Making call from Basic Phone" +brand); } protected String getBrand (){ return brand ; } } public class TestPhone { public static void main(String[] args ) { BasicPhone myPhone = new BasicPhone () ; myPhone.makeCall (); } }

Part 2: Child Class with Method Overriding Create a class named SmartPhone that extends BasicPhone . Override the public method makeCall (). The overridden method must print exactly : Making a call using smartphone features No other methods or variables should be added. Part 3 : Update the `Main` class. Declare a reference variable of type ` SmartPhone `. Assign it an object of ` SmartPhone `. Call the ` makeCall ()` method using this reference. Part 4: Demonstrating Polymorphism Using Parent Reference Update the TestPhone class again. Declare a reference variable of type BasicPhone . Assign this reference variable an object of SmartPhone . Call the makeCall () method using the BasicPhone reference.

class BasicPhone { private String brand = "iPhone" ; protected void makeCall (){ System. out .println ("Making call from Basic Phone" +brand); } protected String getBrand (){ return brand ; } } class SmartPhone extends BasicPhone { @Override protected void makeCall (){ System. out .println ("Making call from Smart Phone" + getBrand ()); } } public class TestPhone { public static void main(String[] args ) { BasicPhone myPhone = new BasicPhone () ; myPhone.makeCall (); SmartPhone mySPhone = new SmartPhone (); mySPhone.makeCall (); } }

Part 4: Demonstrating Polymorphism Using Parent Reference Update the TestPhone class again. Declare a reference variable of type BasicPhone . Assign this reference variable an object of SmartPhone . Call the makeCall () method using the BasicPhone reference.

class BasicPhone { private String brand = "iPhone" ; protected void makeCall (){ System. out .println ("Making call from Basic Phone" +brand); } protected String getBrand (){ return brand ; } } class SmartPhone extends BasicPhone { @Override protected void makeCall (){ System. out .println ("Making call from Smart Phone" + getBrand ()); } } public class TestPhone { public static void main(String[] args ) { BasicPhone myPhone = new BasicPhone () ; myPhone.makeCall (); SmartPhone mySPhone = new SmartPhone (); mySPhone.makeCall (); BasicPhone mySBPhone = new SmartPhone (); mySBPhone.makeCall (); } }

Key Points of Arrays in Java An array is a fixed-size collection of elements of the same data type. Array access is fast because elements are accessed using index values. Array elements are stored in contiguous memory locations. Array size is decided at creation time and cannot be changed later. Arrays use zero-based indexing (first element at index `0`). Arrays in Java are objects, not primitive variables. The `length` property gives the size of the array (not a method). Arrays are created using the `new` keyword. Java automatically initializes array elements with default values. Primitive arrays store values directly, whereas object arrays store references to objects.

SET 1: PRIMITIVE ARRAY (int, double, char, etc.) Declare Array <type> < arrayName >[]; e.g. int x[]; Allocate Memory to array < arrayName > = new <type>[<size>]; e.g. x = new int[100]; Define and allocate memory together <type> < arrayName >[] = new <type>[<size>]; e.g. int x[] = new int[100]; Initialize Array: Option 1 < arrayName >[<subscript>] = <value>; e.g. x[5] = 100; Using loop: For (int i = 0; i < 100; i ++) x[ i ] = <value>; Initialize Array: Option 2 <type> < arrayName >[] = { <list of values> }; e.g. int x[] = {12, 13, 14}; Size of array int i = x.length ; SET 2: OBJECT ARRAY (class type) Declare Array < className > < arrayName >[]; e.g. Book books[]; Allocate Memory to array (references only) < arrayName > = new < className >[<size>]; e.g. books = new Book[3]; Define and allocate memory together < className > < arrayName >[] = new < className >[<size>]; e.g. Book books[] = new Book[3]; Initialize Array: Option 1 (IMPORTANT object creation required) < arrayName >[<index>] = new < className >(); books[0] = new Book(); books[1] = new Book(); Assign values to object fields: books[0].title = "Java"; books[1].title = "OOP"; Initialize Array: Option 2 (array + objects together) < className > < arrayName >[] = { new < className >(), new < className >() }; e.g.Book books[] = { new Book(), new Book() }; Size of array int i = books.length ;

Write a Java program that: Declares an integer array Allocates memory for the array (size 5) Initializes the array elements with any integer values Prints all array elements using a loop Create a class named IntArrayDemo . Define the main method as the program entry point. Inside main, declare an integer array variable. Allocate memory to the array for 5 integer elements. Initialize the array elements with integer values using a loop. Use a loop to access each array element. Print each element to the console. Declare Int Array : int[] x ; Allocate memory: x = new int[5]; Initialize Array: for (int i =0; i < x.length;i ++) X[ i ]= i + 1; mportantYears = new int[] {800, 1066, 1492,};

class IntArrayDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { int[] x = new int[5]; for (int i = 0; i < x.length ; i ++) { x[ i ] = i + 1; } for (int i = 0; i < x.length ; i ++) { System.out.println (x[ i ]); } } }

Primitive Array Specific Points Primitive arrays store actual values (int, double, char, etc.). Only one `new` operation is required. Default values: `int` `0` `double` `0.0` ` boolean ` `false` e.g int[] a = new int[3]; Object Array Specific Points Object arrays store references to objects, not the objects themselves. Creating an object array does not create objects automatically. Each object must be created separately using `new`. Default value for object references is `null`. Accessing an object before creation causes ` NullPointerException `. Example: Book[] books = new Book[2]; books[0] = new Book();

class TestArray { String title ; public static void main(String args []) { TestArray [] myBooks = new TestArray [2]; int x = 0; myBooks [0].title = "b1"; myBooks [1].title = "b2"; while(x < 2) { System. out .println ( "book "+ myBooks [x].title ); x++ ; } } class Hobbits { String name ; public static void main(String args []) { Hobbits[] h = new Hobbits[2] ; int z = 0 ; while (z < 2) { z = z + 1 ; h[z] = new Hobbits() ; h[z].name ="H" + z ; System. out .println ("name "+ h[z].name ); } } }

Program Requirements Object Array Declares an object array Allocates memory for the array (size 5) Creates objects inside the array Prints all object values using a loop Low Level Design Object Array Create a class named Book Add one data member title Create a class named ObjectArrayDemo Define the main method Inside main, declare an object array Allocate memory for 5 objects Create objects using a loop Assign values to object fields Use a loop to print object values Class Creation class Book { String title; } Declare Object Array Book[] books; Allocate Memory books = new Book[5]; Initialize Array, Assign and Read Values for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { books[ i ] = new Book(); books[ i ].title = "Book " + ( i + 1); } for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { System.out.println (books[ i ].title); }

class Book { String title; } class ObjectArrayDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { Book[] books; books = new Book[5]; for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { books[ i ] = new Book(); books[ i ].title = "Book " + ( i + 1); } for (int i = 0; i < books.length ; i ++) { System.out.println (books[ i ].title); } } }
Abstract class Bike{ Abstract void run(); } Class Honda extends Bike{ void run() { System.out.println ( run safely ); } Public static void main() { Bike obj = new Honda() ; obj.run () ; }

A stack stores data using first-in, last-out ordering. Stacks are controlled through two operations traditionally called push and pop . Class: Stack Data Members array stck of size 10 integer tos // top of stack index Constructor: Stack() set tos = -1 // stack is initially empty Method: push(item) if tos equals 9 then print "Stack is full" else increment tos by 1 store item in stck [ tos ] end if Method: pop() if tos is less than 0 then print "Stack underflow" return 0 else store stck [ tos ] in value decrement tos by 1 return value end if Class: TestStack Method: main create object mystack1 of Stack create object mystack2 of Stack Push values into stacks for i from 0 to 9 do mystack1.push( i ) end for for i from 10 to 19 do mystack2.push( i ) end for Pop and display values print "Stack in mystack1:" for i from 0 to 9 do print mystack1.pop() end for print "Stack in mystack2:" for i from 0 to 9 do print mystack2.pop() end for

Integer Array (Creation & Access) Declare and create in one line int[] arr = new int[10]; Store a value in array arr [index] = value; Read a value from array value = arr [index]; Printing Output Print text or value (new line) System.out.println (value); Print text with variable System.out.println ("Text " + variable); Assignment = Arithmetic + addition - subtraction * multiplication / division % modulus Increment & Decrement Pre-increment ++x // increase first, then use Post-increment x++ // use first, then increase Pre-decrement --x Post-decrement x-- Used in stack example: stck [++ tos ] = item; return stck [ tos --]; Comparison Operators (Conditions) == equal to != not equal to < less than > greater than <= less than or equal to >= greater than or equal to Example: if ( tos == 9) if ( tos < 0)

if else Statement if (condition) { statements; } else { statements; } for Loop for (initialization; condition; update) { statements; } Example: for (int i = 0; i < 10; i ++) Method Definition & Call returnType methodName (parameters) Example: void push(int item) int pop() Call method object.method (arguments); Object Creation ClassName objectName = new ClassName (); Example: Stack mystack1 = new Stack(); Key Keywords Used class define a class int integer type void no return value return send value back new create object

The stack size must be received as a parameter through the constructor. Create the stack array using the passed size. The stack should still: Prevent overflow Prevent underflow Use tos to track the top of the stack Test Class Create two Stack objects with different sizes . Push values up to the defined size. Pop and display all values from each stack.

Paper 1 : Question 1: Student Marks Analyzer Write a Java program to store and analyze the marks of a student in 5 subjects using object-oriented principles. Design a class named Student that represents a student. Each student has: a name marks in 5 subjects The Student class must: store its data using private instance variables use a constructor to initialize the student s name and marks provide one getter method named getName () to return the student s name include the following methods: getTotalMarks () returns the total of marks in all 5 subjects getAverageMarks () returns the average marks Direct access to instance variables from outside the class must not be allowed. Create a separate test class containing the main() method. In the main() method: use the Scanner class to read the student s name read marks for 5 subjects into an integer array create a Student object using the constructor display the student s name, total marks, and average marks

1. Scanner Package: ` java.util ` Reads input token by token Easy and beginner-friendly Example methods: ` nextInt ()`, ` nextLine ()`, ` nextDouble ()` 2. BufferedReader Package: `java.io` Used with ` InputStreamReader ` Reads input line by line Faster than Scanner Requires manual type conversion 3. Command Line Arguments Uses `String[] args ` in `main` Input passed while running the program No runtime input 4. Console Class Package: `java.io` Reads input securely (e.g., passwords) Not supported in some IDEs

Scanner sc = new Scanner(System. in ); System. out .print ("Enter your name: "); String name = sc.nextLine (); Scanner is a built-in Java class used to read input from different sources such as keyboard input, files, and strings It belongs to the package java.util When used with System.in, it reads standard input (keyboard input)

Reading String / text input Read a full line (including spaces) String s = sc.nextLine (); Reads: Hello Java World Read a single word (stops at space) String s = sc.next (); Input: Hello Java Stored: Hello Reading integer numbers Read an int int x = sc.nextInt (); Read a long long x = sc.nextLong (); Read a short short x = sc.nextShort (); Read a byte byte x = sc.nextByte (); Reading decimal numbers Read a float float f = sc.nextFloat (); Closing Scanner sc.close (); -- Frees resources

Scanner can read input from many different sources , not just the keyboard. 1. Keyboard input Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); 2. File input Scanner sc = new Scanner(new File("data.txt")); 3. String input (very common in testing) Scanner sc = new Scanner("Java is powerful"); 4. Network / stream input InputStream stream = socket.getInputStream (); Scanner sc = new Scanner(stream);

It represents an is-a relationship (e.g., Car is a Vehicle). It promotes code reusability by avoiding duplication. The child class can use, extend, or override methods of the parent class. Access to parent members depends on access modifiers (public, protected, etc.).

Printing directly () System.out.println (temp); // shows reference, not data // 3. import java.util.Arrays ; public class TwoDArrayDemo { pubic static void main(String[] args ) { // 1. 2D array creation int[][] temp = { {30, 31, 29}, {32, 33, 31}, {28, 27, 29} }; System.out.println ("Printing using Arrays utility:"); // row by row (1D at a time) for (int i = 0; i < temp.length ; i ++) { System.out.println ( Arrays.toString (temp[ i ])); } System.out.println ("Full 2D print using deepToString :"); System.out.println ( Arrays.deepToString (temp)); } } 2-D Array java.util.Arrays

import java.util.Arrays ; public class JaggedArrayDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { int[][] temp = { {30, 31}, {32, 33, 31}, {28} }; System.out.println ("Row-wise printing:"); for (int i = 0; i < temp.length ; i ++) { System.out.println ( Arrays.toString (temp[ i ])); } // Printing full jagged array using deepToString () System.out.println ("Full jagged array:"); System.out.println ( Arrays.deepToString (temp)); } } 2-D Jagged Array java.util.Arrays

A class teacher has collected the marks of students in an exam. The teacher wants to: Arrange the marks in ascending order Find whether a particular student s mark exists Increase the capacity to store more marks Check whether two mark lists are the same sort : teacher arranges marks in order binarySearch : teacher checks if a student s mark exists copyOf : teacher prepares space for new students equals : teacher compares two result sheets 2-D Arrays java.util methods

import java.util.Arrays ; class StudentMarksArray { public static void main(String[] args ) { // 1. Create array and store marks int[] marks = {72, 85, 64, 90, 78}; // 2. Sort marks in ascending order Arrays.sort (marks); // 3. Search for a particular student's mark int searchMark = 78; int result = Arrays.binarySearch (marks, searchMark ); if (result >= 0) { System.out.println ("Mark found at index: " + result); } else { System.out.println ("Mark not found"); } } } 2-D Arrays java.util code

class TeacherMarksArrayResize { public static void main(String[] args ) { // Initial capacity for marks int[] marks = new int[2]; int size = 0; // number of marks actually stored // Adding marks marks[size++] = 72; marks[size++] = 85; // Capacity full need resizing int[] newMarks = new int[10]; System.arraycopy (marks, 0, newMarks , 0, marks.length ); marks = newMarks ; marks[size++] = 55; } } Resize class

import java.util.ArrayList ; class TeacherMarksResize { public static void main(String[] args ) { ArrayList <Integer> marks = new ArrayList <>(); marks.ensureCapacity (10); marks.add (72); marks.add (85); marks.ensureCapacity (20); marks.add (55); marks.add (88); System.out.println ("Total students: " + marks.size ()); } } Resize class with Arraylist

Build a University CRM program to 1. Store student & employee information 2. manage student records, calculate averages, sort by performance, and handle dynamic additions.
Part 1 Store University Information

A university has different types of people. Every person in the university has: a name an ID There are two main groups of people: Students They are enrolled in courses. They belong to a department. Each student has marks in 5 subjects. Employees They work for the university. They belong to a department. There are different kinds of employees: Teachers : Teachers teach courses. Administrative Staff : Administrative Staff handle university operations. General Notes: Teachers, Students, and Administrative Staff may conduct research. There are also Departments in the university. Each department has: department name department code
Class Just an object Subclass More specific version Abstract Class Common template Interface Role anyone can play

1 Person name : String id : int 2 Student name : String id : int Marks : int[] departmentName : String 3 Employee name : String id : int departmentName : String 4 Teacher name : String id : int departmentName : String 5 AdministrativeStaff name : String id : int departmentName : String 6 Department departmentName : String departmentCode : String 7 Researcher (optional) (No attributes)

1 Abstract Class A class that provides common structure. We should not create its object directly. It represents a general idea, not a concrete entity. It reduces duplication in child classes. 2 Subclass A more specific version of another class. Must pass the IS-A test. Inherits properties from a parent class. 3 Interface Represents a role or ability. Different unrelated classes can implement it. It defines behavior, not identity. 4 Normal Class A regular concrete object. Does not act as a template. Does not represent a role. Can be instantiated directly.

Part 2 Student Marks Sorting Build a Java program to manage student records, calculate averages, sort by performance, and handle dynamic additions. The program will be implemented in two stages : Stage A Basic Student Creation Create the Student class. Use hard-coded data to create student objects. Store them in an Array / Arraylist ?? Display all student details including calculated average. Stage B Sorting by Performance Implement Comparable<Student>. Override compareTo () to sort students by average marks. Use Collections.sort () to rank students. Display the sorted results.
Compare & Sort

import java.util.Arrays ; class Student implements Comparable<Student> { int id; String name; double marks; public Student(int id, String name, double marks) { this.id = id; this.name = name; this.marks = marks; } // compare by marks (highest first) @Override public int compareTo (Student s) { if ( this.marks < s.marks ) return 1; else if ( this.marks > s.marks ) return -1; else return 0; } public void display() { System.out.println (id + " " + name + " " + marks); } } Student Class with compareTo Using Marks

public class Main { public static void main(String[] args ) { // Create array of Students Student[] arr = new Student[5]; arr [0] = new Student(1, "Aman", 85); arr [1] = new Student(2, "Riya", 92); arr [2] = new Student(3, "Karan", 78); arr [3] = new Student(4, "Neha", 88); arr [4] = new Student(5, "Arjun", 95); System.out.println ("Before Sorting:"); for (Student s : arr ) { s.display (); } // Sort array Arrays.sort ( arr ); System.out.println ("\ nAfter Sorting (Highest Marks First):"); for (Student s : arr ) { s.display (); } } } Main Class implementation with Arrays

import java.util.ArrayList ; import java.util.Collections ; public class Main { public static void main(String[] args ) { // Create ArrayList of Students ArrayList <Student> list = new ArrayList <>(); list.add (new Student(1, "Aman", 85)); list.add (new Student(2, "Riya", 92)); list.add (new Student(3, "Karan", 78)); list.add (new Student(4, "Neha", 88)); list.add (new Student(5, "Arjun", 95)); System.out.println ("Before Sorting:"); for (Student s : list) { s.display (); } // Sort ArrayList Collections.sort (list); System.out.println ("\ nAfter Sorting (Highest Marks First):"); for (Student s : list) { s.display (); } } } Main Class implementation with ArrayList

class Course implements Comparable<Course> { int courseId ; String courseName ; double marks1; double marks2; double marks3; // Constructor public Course(int courseId , String courseName , double marks1, double marks2, double marks3) { this.courseId = courseId ; this.courseName = courseName ; this.marks1 = marks1; this.marks2 = marks2; this.marks3 = marks3; } // Method to calculate average public double getAverage () { return (marks1 + marks2 + marks3) / 3; } // compareTo based on average (highest first) Student Class with compareTo Using Marks Average @Override public int compareTo (Course c) { double thisAvg = this.getAverage (); double otherAvg = c.getAverage (); if ( thisAvg < otherAvg ) return 1; else if ( thisAvg > otherAvg ) return -1; else return 0; } // Display method public void display() { System.out.println ( courseId + " " + courseName + " Avg: " + getAverage ()); } }

import java.util.ArrayList ; import java.util.Collections ; public class Main { public static void main(String[] args ) { // Create ArrayList of Course objects ArrayList <Course> list = new ArrayList <>(); // Add 5 courses list.add (new Course(1, "Math", 80, 90, 85)); list.add (new Course(2, "Physics", 75, 88, 82)); list.add (new Course(3, "Chemistry", 92, 85, 90)); list.add (new Course(4, "Biology", 70, 78, 74)); list.add (new Course(5, "English", 85, 87, 89)); System.out.println ("Before Sorting:"); for (Course c : list) { c.display (); } // Sort based on average (highest first) Collections.sort (list); System.out.println ("\ nAfter Sorting (Based on Average):"); for (Course c : list) { c.display (); } } }

Collection (Interface) Represents a group of objects. Defines basic operations: add() remove() size() contains() isEmpty () Does NOT define order or indexing. List (Interface) A specialized type of Collection. Maintains insertion order. Allows duplicates. Provides index-based access: get(index) set(index, element) add(index, element)

class Student implements Comparable<Student> { String name; int sapId ; int marks; // Constructor Student(String name, int sapId , int marks) { this.name = name; this.sapId = sapId ; this.marks = marks; } // compareTo defines natural ordering (by marks) public int compareTo (Student s) { return this.marks - s.marks ; // ascending order } }

public class ComparableDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { Student[] students = new Student[3]; students[0] = new Student("Amit", 101, 78); students[1] = new Student("Neha", 102, 85); students[2] = new Student("Rahul", 103, 72); // Arrays uses compareTo () internally Arrays.sort (students); // Display sorted students for (Student s : students) { System.out.println (s.name + " " + s.sapId + " " + s.marks ); } } }
Part 4

Online Food Delivery App : The system must handle: Customers place orders throughout the day. Each order has a unique Order ID. Orders are prepared in the kitchen in the order they arrive. The app maintains a list of unique restaurant names registered on the platform.

class Jukebox1 { public static void main(String[] args ) { // Instantiate an object of HashSet HashSet<Integer> hs = new HashSet<>(); // Adding elements hs.add (1); hs.add (2); hs.add (1); System. out .println ("HashSet Size: " + hs.size ()); System. out .println ("Elements in HashSet: " + hs ); } } What will the size be ??

import java.util.HashSet ; class StudentHash { int id; String name; StudentHash (int id, String name) { this.id = id; this.name = name; } } public class StudentDemoHash { public static void main(String args []) { HashSet< StudentHash > set = new HashSet<>(); StudentHash s1 = new StudentHash (1, "Ram"); StudentHash s2 = new StudentHash (1, "Ram"); set.add (s1); set.add (s2); System. out .println ( set.size ()); } }

import java.util.HashSet ; import java.util.Objects ; class StudentHash { int id; String name; StudentHash (int id, String name) { this.id = id; this.name = name; } // Equality based ONLY on id @Override public boolean equals(Object obj) { if (this == obj) return true; if (obj == null || getClass () != obj.getClass ()) return false; StudentHash other = ( StudentHash ) obj; return this.id == other.id; } @Override public int hashCode () { return Objects.hash (id); } public class StudentDemoHash { public static void main(String args []) { HashSet< StudentHash > set = new HashSet<>(); StudentHash s1 = new StudentHash (1, "Ram"); StudentHash s2 = new StudentHash (1, "Ram"); set.add (s1); set.add (s2); System.out.println ( set.size ()); // Output: 1 } }

import java.util.ArrayList ; import java.util.HashSet ; public class CopyExample { public static void main(String[] args ) { // Step 1: Create ArrayList ArrayList <String> studentList = new ArrayList <>(); studentList.add ("Aman"); studentList.add ("Riya"); studentList.add ("Aman"); // duplicate studentList.add ("Karan"); System.out.println (" ArrayList :"); System.out.println ( studentList ); // Step 2: Create HashSet HashSet<String> studentSet = new HashSet<>(); // Step 3: Copy using addAll () studentSet.addAll ( studentList ); System.out.println ("HashSet:"); System.out.println ( studentSet ); } }
Multi Level Inheritance
Super this

Multi Level Inheritance A manufacturing company wants to design a simple system to represent different roles of individuals working in the organization. Every individual in the company is first considered a Person . Some persons work as Factory Workers . Some persons work as Managers . Among managers, a few are promoted to Directors . Each class must define a parameterized constructor to initialize its instance variables. Subclasses must invoke the parent class constructor using the super keyword. Use the this keyword to assign values to instance variables within constructors. Define a method named introduceRole () in the base class. All subclasses must override the introduceRole () method to reflect role-specific behavior. In the main method, create objects using a Person reference to demonstrate runtime polymorphism

Class: Person Type: Base Class Variables: name (String) age (int) Methods: Constructor to initialize variables introduceRole () Class: FactoryWorker Extends: Person Additional Variable: shift (String) Methods: Constructor (initialize all variables) Override introduceRole () Class: Manager Extends: Person Additional Variable: department (String) Methods: Constructor (initialize all variables) Override introduceRole () Class: Director Extends: Manager Additional Variable: annualBonus (double) Methods: Constructor (initialize all variables) Override introduceRole ()

// Base Class abstract class Person { protected String name; protected int age; public Person(String name, int age) { } public abstract void introduceRole (); } // First Level Specialization class FactoryWorker extends Person { private String shift; public FactoryWorker (String name, int age, String shift) { } @Override public void introduceRole () { } } // First Level Specialization class Manager extends Person { protected String department; public Manager(String name, int age, String department) { } @Override public void introduceRole () { } } // Multilevel Specialization class Director extends Manager { private double annualBonus ; public Director(String name, int age, String department, double annualBonus ) { } @Override public void introduceRole () { } } // Driver Class public class OrganizationalRoleDemo { public static void main(String[] args ) { Person p1 = new FactoryWorker (...); Person p2 = new Manager(...); Person p3 = new Director(...); p1.introduceRole(); p2.introduceRole(); p3.introduceRole(); } }

abstract class Person { protected String name; protected int age; // Constructor public Person(String name, int age) { this.name = name; // using this this.age = age; System. out .println ("Person constructor executed"); } // Abstract method (forces overriding) public abstract void introduceRole (); }

class FactoryWorker extends Person { private String shift; // Constructor public FactoryWorker (String name, int age, String shift) { super (name, age); // calling parent constructor this .shift = shift; // using this System. out .println (" FactoryWorker constructor executed"); } @ Override public void introduceRole () { System. out .println ("I am " + name + ", a Factory Worker working in the " + shift + " shift."); } }

class Manager extends Person { protected String department; // Constructor public Manager(String name, int age, String department) { super(name, age); // calling parent constructor this.department = department; System. out .println ("Manager constructor executed"); } @Override public void introduceRole () { System. out .println ("I am " + name + ", managing the " + department + " department."); } }

class Director extends Manager { private double annualBonus ; // Constructor public Director(String name, int age, String department, double annualBonus ) { super(name, age, department); // calling Manager constructor this.annualBonus = annualBonus ; System. out .println ("Director constructor executed"); } @Override public void introduceRole () { System. out .println ("I am " + name + ", Director of " + department + " department with an annual bonus of " + annualBonus + "."); } }

Abstraction Can we create an object of Person if it is declared as abstract? Why? What happens if I remove abstract from introduceRole () in Person but still override it in subclasses? If I remove the abstract keyword from the class but keep the abstract method inside it, what happens? Constructor & Inheritance If I create a Director object, what will be the exact print order? Why does the parent constructor execute before the child constructor? What happens if I try to place super(...) after another statement inside the constructor? Polymorphism: If I remove the overridden method from Director, which method will execute? Encapsulation: What happens if I make all variables public? If I make introduceRole () private in Person, can subclasses override it? If I make introduceRole () final in Person, what happens?

Main Method Requirements Create objects using Person reference: Person p1 = new FactoryWorker (" ravi kumar ", 28, "Night Shift"); Person p2 = new Manager(" anita sharma ", 40, "Production Planning"); Person p3 = new Director(" mohit verma ", 52, "Corporate Strategy", 750000); p1.introduceRole(); p2.introduceRole(); p3.introduceRole();
Backup

class Phone { String brand = "Samsung"; class Battery { void show() { System.out.println ("Battery of " + brand); } } public static void main(String[] args ) { Phone p = new Phone(); Phone.Battery b = p.new Battery(); b.show (); } } Member Inner Class

class Phone { static String type = "Smartphone"; static class SIM { void show() { System.out.println ("Type: " + type); } } public static void main(String[] args ) { Phone.SIM s = new Phone.SIM (); // no outer object needed s.show (); } } Static Nested Class

class Phone { void start() { class Screen { void show() { System.out.println ("Screen ON"); } } Screen s = new Screen(); // only inside this method s.show (); } public static void main(String[] args ) { Phone p = new Phone(); p.start (); } } Local Inner Class
class Phone { public static void main(String[] args ) { Runnable r = new Runnable() { public void run() { System.out.println ("Phone ringing"); } }; r.run (); } } Anonymous Inner Class

______ nested classes are called inner classes. A) Static B) Abstract C) Non-static D) Final To create an object of a member inner class: A) new Outer.Inner () B) Outer.Inner obj = new Inner(); C) Outer.Inner obj = outerObj.new Inner(); D) new Inner(); A static nested class: A) Requires outer class object B) Can access only static members of outer class C) Cannot be instantiated D) Must be private A local inner class is declared: A) Inside another class B) Inside a method C) Inside a package D) Inside interface only

Anonymous inner class: A) Has no name B) Cannot implement interfaces C) Cannot override methods D) Must be static Anonymous inner class is generally used to: A) Create reusable components B) Override a method once C) Extend multiple classes D) Replace abstract class completely Which of the following requires an outer class object? A) Static nested class B) Anonymous class C) Member inner class D) Interface Which nested class cannot be accessed outside the method in which it is declared? A) Static nested class B) Member inner class C) Local inner class D) Anonymous class

Explain and provide examples of the following types of nested classes in Java: Member Inner Class Anonymous Inner Class Local Inner Class Static Nested Class For each type, explain its characteristics, how it can access the outer class s members, and provide a short code snippet demonstrating its usage. Discuss the key differences between static and non-static nested classes.
